The word آوَوْا (awaw) means to provide protection and refuge—it comes from the same root as مَأْوَى (ma'wa), a place of refuge and perfect protection. These are the Ansar of Madinah who opened their homes, shared their wealth, and supported the Muhajirun.

If this were one unified group, the ayah could have simply continued with conjunctions. But by using two separate definite articles, Allah ﷻ makes them two distinct groups with their own characteristics and contributions.

"Those—all of them—are protective friends and allies of one another." The word أَوْلِيَاء indicates the deepest form of loyalty and protective friendship. Both groups, though distinct, have ultimate loyalty to each other.

Then comes a challenging statement: "And those who believed but did not emigrate—you owe no loyalty to them at all until they make hijrah."

There were people in Makkah who claimed faith but refused to make the sacrifice of hijrah. Perhaps they turned back from Islam. Perhaps they weren't willing to make the necessary sacrifice. The ayah establishes that you owe them nothing. Even if they call themselves Muslims, you are not to trust them in any way—not until they make hijrah themselves.

But there's an important exception. Among those who didn't migrate, there are two categories: those who didn't want to migrate, and those who wanted to but couldn't. Perhaps they were caught, captured, held back by family, lacked resources, or faced other obstacles. The ayah separates these two groups.

"However, if they seek your help in matters of religion, then helping them becomes obligatory upon you." If someone in Makkah who wanted to migrate but couldn't now asks for help—perhaps they want to escape but need assistance—then you must help them. You must come to their aid.

"Except against a people between whom and you there is a treaty." If helping them would require military action against a group with whom you have a peace agreement, you cannot violate that treaty. Help means going militarily to extract them, like a rescue mission. You will undertake no military activity against any tribe or group with whom you have an existing agreement.

"And Allah ﷻ is Basir (fully seeing) of what you do."

These ethics of war are revealed at the very moment of war itself. This is remarkable. When war happens, rules typically go out the window. There's an expression: "All is fair in love and war." Practically speaking, everything becomes lawless. There are no Geneva Conventions on the battlefield. When anger takes over, entire villages can be annihilated without a second thought.

A soldier on the battlefield, especially when alone or with close comrades away from oversight, fears only two things: that one of his own will report him, or that a superior might see. That's his only restraint. Soldiers don't follow rules because they love the rules—they often hate the restrictions. Whenever they believe the cameras aren't watching, they do what they want. This is true across the board, regardless of nationality. There are noble soldiers occasionally, but generally, when human beings receive an open license to do whatever they want, they do exactly that.

The Muslim soldier operates from an entirely different framework. He knows Allah ﷻ is watching him constantly, at all times, in everything he does. If he's conscious of that reality, he cannot act as other soldiers act. He cannot simply exact revenge when no superior officers are around. There are limits on him—internal limits. He doesn't think, "No one's watching, so I can do whatever I want." The word of Allah ﷻ isn't like a constitution or military code sitting on a shelf somewhere. It's the word of Allah ﷻ being recorded by angels, with Allah ﷻ Himself watching at all times.

This phrase "And Allah ﷻ is Seeing of what you do" is strategically placed. Other soldiers don't have that sense of authority over them. The Muslim soldier cannot escape divine oversight.

translation: And those who disbelieved are allies of one another. If you do not do so, there will be fitnah on earth and great corruption
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءُ بَعْضٍ ۚ إِلَّا تَفْعَلُوهُ تَكُن فِتْنَةٌ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَفَسَادٌ كَبِيرٌ

And those who disbelieved are allies of one another. If you do not do this, there will be fitnah on earth and great corruption.

Just as the believers have formed alliances and bonds of loyalty, "those who disbelieve are also protective allies and loyal to one another."

Then comes the warning: "If you do not do this"—if you don't come to the aid of your people when they ask for help—"there will be great trial and tribulation in the land, and huge corruption will spread."

What is this corruption? Muslims who were weak, who couldn't make hijrah, will receive no help. What happens to them? They'll abandon Islam, saying, "Why should we remain Muslim? They didn't come to help us." You'll have a population of people who tasted Islam and then deliberately left it. That is enormous fitnah.

There are heartbreaking historical examples of this. In contemporary conflicts between Muslim groups—where political situations are complex and taking sides is difficult—there have been cases where Muslims were killed or abandoned, where Muslim women were captured by armies and assimilated into other cultures. In some cases they were converted to other faiths. Later, movements arose to bring these women back, but many refused. "Where were you when we needed rescuing? What are we supposed to do with you now? We have families, children. We've moved on." That loss represents massive corruption—exactly what this ayah warns against.

translation: But those who have believed and emigrated and fought in the cause of Allah and those who gave shelter and aided - it is they who are the believers, truly. For them is forgiveness and noble provision
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَهَاجَرُوا۟ وَجَـٰهَدُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَاوَوا۟ وَّنَصَرُوٓا۟ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ حَقًّا ۚ لَّهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَرِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ

And those who have believed and emigrated and fought in the cause of Allah ﷻ and those who gave shelter and aided—it is they who are the believers, truly. For them is forgiveness and noble provision.

Allah ﷻ returns to describing the two groups: those who believed, migrated and struggled in Allah's path (the Muhajirun), and those who provided shelter and help (the Ansar).

"Those are the true believers. Actually, those are the real believers."

Where have we heard this phrase before? In the second ayah of this very surah: "The believers are only those who, when Allah ﷻ is mentioned, their hearts tremble, and when His verses are recited to them, it increases them in faith, and upon their Lord they rely."

That early ayah described the personal, spiritual dimension of faith—the individual's relationship with Allah ﷻ. There are five pillars of Islam that represent individual responsibilities: shahada, salah, zakah, fasting, and hajj.

But these believers in ayah 74 were part of a larger mission. That mission demanded that once they had iman, they had to make hijrah when the time came. Iman called them to jihad in Allah's path. On the other side, iman compelled the Ansar to provide shelter and support. They were all part of the greater mission of giving victory to Islam in the region.

So there's the personal side of the believer—when ayahs are recited, their iman increases, they put their trust in their Lord. That's one dimension. Then there's the side of the believer where he's part of a larger cause, part of the Messenger's ﷺ work. Allah ﷻ says these are the true believers—acknowledging both dimensions.

Is it possible for someone who didn't make hijrah, sitting in Makkah, to shed tears when Quran is recited? Absolutely. That's one side of the coin. But Allah ﷻ presents both sides now. The other side of the coin is that you're part of a larger mission, a greater task. You cannot be merely a spiritual person while neglecting responsibility for the collective work. There's real work to be done.

In the modern Muslim world, we've created a divorce between Islamic activism and spirituality. You have people of spirituality on one side and people of Islamic activism on the other. Either you're involved in educational work, establishing schools, community organizing—you're on a mission—or you say all of that is empty, we need to purify ourselves, we've become so negligent, we have sins to address, we need to do dhikr, and you become a person focused entirely on personal worship.

The Quran doesn't allow you to separate these two dimensions. What's interesting is that activists often view spirituality as weak, as though it takes away from real work: "These people just sit and do dhikr while we're actually doing something." On the other hand, spiritual people say, "These activists have no heart, they're empty, they're walking shells with no reality to their efforts. We've tasted the sweetness of iman."

And yes, tasting the sweetness of tears in salah—experiencing genuine joy from dhikr—is powerfully addictive in the best way. If you find joy in remembrance of Allah ﷻ, it's stronger than any worldly pleasure. But look at the Prophet's ﷺ own life. He was commanded to stand in prayer half the night. He was also commanded to leave that prayer and go warn people. What do you think he enjoyed more? Obviously standing in prayer, conversing with Allah ﷻ. What greater joy exists than speaking with the Creator? And what brings more pain than addressing obstinate people who insult you, who insult Allah ﷻ and His book constantly, who only grow worse?

Yet you must have both. That's the complete personality of the believer. We've imposed our own preferences and backgrounds onto the religion, making certain aspects our priority. But the Quran doesn't let you choose. It maintains both dimensions together—just as in Surah Ali 'Imran, where the intellectual and spiritual were balanced. Here, struggle and remembrance are balanced.

One beautiful description of the companions was: "They were riding horses in the day and praying all night." That's who they were—on the mission during the day, connected to Allah ﷻ at night. You cannot abandon one for the other.

"For them is forgiveness and noble provision"—maghfirah and rizq karim—addressing this complete group of true believers.

translation: And those who believed after [the initial emigration] and emigrated and fought with you - they are of you. But those of [blood] relationship are more entitled [to inheritance] in the decree of Allah. Indeed, Allah is Knowing of all things
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدُ وَهَاجَرُوا۟ وَجَـٰهَدُوا۟ مَعَكُمْ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مِنكُمْ ۚ وَأُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَرْحَامِ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلَىٰ بِبَعْضٍ فِى كِتَـٰبِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌۢ

And those who believed after and emigrated and fought with you—they are of you. But those of blood relationship are more entitled to inheritance in the decree of Allah ﷻ. Indeed, Allah ﷻ is Knowing of all things.

"And those who came to iman afterwards"—people like Khalid ibn al-Walid (RA), who embraced Islam after the early community was established—"and then they eventually made hijrah and struggled with you, then they are among you." Don't think of them as lesser. The promise of maghfirah and rizq karim extends to them as well.

But Allah ﷻ immediately adds a crucial qualification: "But don't take this brotherhood too far and start applying legal rulings based on spiritual brotherhood."

When the Prophet ﷺ established brotherhood between the Muhajirun and Ansar, it created such powerful bonds that the companions were ready to extend it to inheritance. Your brother in Islam would share your inheritance just like blood family. Allah ﷻ had to intervene and establish limits.

"Those of blood relationship (ulu al-arham)—blood relations—take priority when it comes to applying the law of Allah ﷻ in matters of inheritance." Don't make your brothers in faith shares in inheritance based solely on spiritual bonds. There are stories of companions wanting to divorce their wives so a single brother could marry her, or offering half their house and property. Allah ﷻ says: No, pull back. They're your brothers in wilayah (protective alliance and loyalty), but they're not your brothers in the legal application found in Allah's book.

"Indeed, Allah ﷻ is 'Alim (completely knowledgeable) of all things."

Consider the remarkable transformation. This surah began with Muslims on the verge of fighting each other over war booty distribution, arguing about who should get what. The surah ends with Muslims so united that Allah ﷻ has to establish boundaries on their love, telling them not to be that united in legal matters. Keep limits on your generosity.

We see both dimensions of the Muslim community in these closing ayahs, and both dimensions of the Muslim personality in the second-to-last ayah. There's the individual, spiritual dimension—the personal relationship with Allah ﷻ. And there's the collective dimension—being part of the mission, the struggle, the work that must be done. Both are essential. Both define true iman. Neither can be abandoned for the other.
